I've been playing Pokemon games since I was 5. I grew up watching the show and collecting cards. 

This game gives you the closest experience to how things work on the shows. The real-time battles and real-time catching are a breath of fresh air to someone who always wanted to experience Pokemon shows.

The graphics are great and the world feels alive. I am frankly pretty confused about all the hate this game has gotten from users. I think for once critics actually are closer to the truth. 

The story does feel flat and the intro is God awful. Pokemon games should come with an option to get a quicker intro if they are long time players. But I am really excited to sink some good hours into this game.
It’s really good and has good combat and nice location and has got good cast of Pokémon and turn based gameplay is good and is good game for 58 British pounds and is good sequel to arceus
Unfinished Game. Trash DLC. Almost like scam. Cost so much as 70 for main and 30 for DLC. I wouldn’t play for free if I know the content.

Have you heard about the new Pokémon game? Pokémon Legends: Z-A. And it’s an absolute disaster. After waiting for so many years for the kind of Pokémon game fans have been asking for, we end up with an extremely poor product, one where not even the visual quality does it any justice. It’s a boring, restrictive game, with no real animations, nothing meaningful to do, set in a city that is also small. And the only aspect that was supposed to be revolutionary—the change to a new combat system instead of classic turn-based battles—ends up being a worse version of turn-based combat, without adding anything new or genuinely good.

I've been a die-hard fan for over 20 years. I've played every game from Red/Blue all the way up to Scarlet/Violet. Legends ZA is the first time I've ever set a Pokémon game down. 

Unlike most people, the graphics and flat textures didn't bug me much. My issue is moreso with the general world design and story. Lumiose City lacks personality. As you progress through the game, you start to realize that the city blends together and each new area you gain access to feels the same as the last. Compare this to any of the GB/DS era games where each city in its region has its own character and reason to be there, and you'll quickly realize how much Game Freak's region design has fallen off in recent entries. My desire to explore quickly fell off, only branching out to find new Wild Areas so I could complete my dex for Home.

The story felt repetitive after a certain point. Visit Wild Areas to build your dex during the day, compete in Battle Zones to earn your Challenger's Ticket at night, repeat. Real-time-battles were a neat addition (when it works), but the novelty of this change alone wasn't enough to keep my enthusiasm up. Finding out that I needed to win 1,000 of these battles in order to obtain the Shiny Charm felt like an unfair and daunting task. Mix in frequent (and often cringeworthy) unskippable dialogue, and you've got a main story that feels like a chore at times to complete.

I had a bad taste put in my mouth minutes into playing the DLC. After previously grinding out battles and only being around 100 away from obtaining the Shiny Charm, I instead was able to get it from simply registering 5 Pokémon in the new dex. Knowing I'll never get the hours spent battling for no reason back feels like a slap in the face. The DLC's story shared the same repetitive nature the main story had. Make some donuts, catch Pokémon, fight a Mega, unskippable dialogue, repeat. A few hours into the DLC, I gave up. I realized I was no longer having fun, and that's the entire reason I play these games in the first place.

With each new entry in the franchise since Black and White feeling progressively worse than the last, I hope we'll see a day where Game Freak cares about quality again. I wish they would either devote the same amount of care to their 3D games as they did their 2D games, or abandon 3D completely. No one should pay $100 for this level of mediocrity, when not that long ago we were receiving masterpieces for $40. Pokémon as a franchise means so much to me and has for the majority of my life, I just want to see them give me a reason to want to pick it up again in the future.

tl;dr : one single town to explore with no varying locales, a story with almost no content drip-fed in between a gameplay loop stretched to the limits of what you'd call thin, no voice acting, a "new" gameplay for battles that's essentially the bare minimum of what you could do to do away with the traditional turn-based system. All for an asking price of 70 bucks.

------------------------------------------

As is usually the case with this franchise, the game can be summed up with "Lots of potential, barely any of it exploited."

The game has a grand total of One (1) town to explore. That's it. No routes, no other towns, no islands, no nothing. The one Paris knock-off is all you get. Hope you like paved streets and PNG buildings because that's all you're going to see!

This makes progressing through the game's "story" incredibly dull. You see the same locales ad nauseam, regardless of what "quest" you're doing. It got so bad that at one point the game gives you a sewer level and I was ELATED. Elated. To see. A sewer level. Just because it was something, ANYTHING other than god. damn. Lumiose.Speaking of the "story", yes, with quotes, it's honestly... pretty basic, and extremely wafer-thin. You're drip-fed minuscule pieces of it in between the gameplay loop of grinding for a challenger ticket, ranking up in the Royale, and fighting rogue Megas. Three things that you will be spending 99% of your game time doing, which very quickly get old. Also, in typical most-profitable-entertainment-franchise-in-the-entire-world fashion, there is no voice acting. Because of course the little multi-billion dollar corporation couldn't possibly afford voice actors. Of course, old school 2D Pokémon game didn't need voice acting, but we're in 2025, the game is in full 3D, and you're trying to sell me a pseudo-RPG story... with mute 3D character models silently flapping their lips..? And you're asking 70 EUROS for it?

And that's honestly the crux of the issue here. Forget this being a Pokemon game for a second. Imagine this was some indie AA RPG, sold for... 20 or 30 bucks. That would honestly be a decent enough time-killer. But that's not the case. This is a first party game, supposed to be a system seller, of the MOST PROFITABLE ENTERTAINMENT FRANCHISE EVER MADE, sold at FULL PRICE. It's just ridiculous.

Oh, and the combat... I appreciate GF finally deciding to modernize the tired old gameplay formula of turn-based combat, but... this needs work, man. And a lot of it. The new combat system essentially boils down to "spam your moves whenever they're off cooldown". Forget coming up with clever strategies with non-damaging moves. None of it matters here. Just slap on the hardest hitting moves you've got and go to town.

Finally, the multiplayer. It's just sad. You get one PvP mode, which is a free-for-all where 4 players are pitting in an arena against each other. Predictably, the strategy is just securing last hits on the others when they're fighting each other, or just one-shotting everything when you have the type advantage. Primo, Boring. Stuff.

And as for trading... All you get is a code system. You input a number, and you get matched with random people using the same code. That's it. You can't post ads for what you're offering and seeking. It's pathetic. You'll have to go on forums or look up youtube videos of popular communities that dedicate specific numbers for specific Pokémon. Have fun.

Now I'd like to dedicate a little segment to what's good about the game, but I'm genuinely coming up blank. I guess every Pokemon having a 3D model and being able to walk around with you is nice, but it's hardly new.

Anyway, I hate metacritic's complete lack of formatting making these reviews barely legible, so I'll stop here. Bad game, not worth the price in a million years. Still waiting for this franchise to change hands for someone to finally do something actually good with it.

Very disappointing. Save your money and hope they do better next time.

The bad first, since there's plenty of it to go around.
- The battle system is extremely clunky. There are clear moves you should use instead of others since pretty much any of the "Ranged" moves require the pokemon walk over to you in the middle of the fight, wasting plenty of precious time as is, to cast it from where you hit the button. It being made for controllers also means swapping between using balls and battling isn't very responsive and the game doesn't even swap off the ball menu half the time when you fail to catch something, so you just take free damage. Not to mention that for some reason the rogue megas just target your character consistently instead of the pokemon they're supposed to be battling. An ambitious attempt, to be sure, but not well done and a new idea done poorly is as bad as any other bad idea.
- The textures and visuals are the same as always. Gamefreak being gamefreak.
- Voice acting still missing, but that's clearly intentional at this point.
- The wild zones are interesting, but ultimately lackluster. The first few show off pretty well what can be done with them, and instead you end up with a mess of halfhearted ideas for them.
- Returning to trade evos in what's supposed to be the single player rpg style line of games just shows they're being pretty greedy about paid online again and think it'll get people paying for it. To their credit, you can largely catch everything still if you shell out for the dlc apparently, but that's not really all that far in their favor considering they paywalled it.
- Very disappointing selection of pokemon for the base game, but that's largely taste based so I'm only including it as the last of the bad ones off the top of my head.

The good, what little there is.
- Models are at least more expressive than they used to be. There were a few times I could actually see what they were trying to express from a character.
- The cast is all fairly interesting for a pokemon game, which surprised me quite a bit to be honest.
- It does feel a lot more like pokemon have integrated into everyday life in this one, which is a huge step up over most games. They're supposed to be peoples partners, but most games don't really lean into it like this one does. People actually seem to care what their pokemon want in this one.
- The new megas are at least funny, if nothing else. Looking at you, Starmie.

So, as always. One step forward, two steps back in classic gamefreak/tpc fashion.

Battling is fun. The world is classic Game Freak crunch. No textures. Pedestrians stand still in a bustling city. The shops are menus. Why bother creating an overworld? This game could have been a menu. 

Story wise, the team is annoying. You do all the heavy lifting and your rival is incredibly self-centered despite being your teammate.

There are games that have a lot of dialogue, but put effort in the story and animations, as well as present to you with interestint or at the very least, relatable characters.

There are games that don't have much content and get repetitive, but at least have out effort into polishing every square inch of the experience.

There are games that are expensive, but at the very least look great and show that the effort and value have gone somewhere.

There are those that respect your time and don't waste it with meandering "plot" and "dialogue."

This game takes the worst aspect in every field and amps it up to 11 to the point that it feels like a parody of what not to do in a game. It is incredible how little I can care about a story and how disrespected I have felt while playing this... Thing.

Like many other Pokémon fans, I really wanted to like this game, but it fell flat in so many disappointing ways. I think they had some great ideas, but somehow almost every part of the game just felt unfinished. For what is supposed to be a AAA title from one of the biggest gaming studios, this was not acceptable for the price they demanded.I liked the switch-up of the battle system, but I think they needed to push it further. Even taking the cooldowns into consideration, it still doesn’t give much reason to pick any movesets you wouldn’t have picked in the other games. You’re still just choosing moves that deal high damage and have type advantage. I wish there were more combo potential, but every move feels sluggish to use because of the weird delay between calling out a move and the Pokémon actually performing it, along with whatever wind-up animation is required. I also feel that trying to access the item menu during battle feels clunky. Maybe a little more user feedback when you try to open the menu and can’t—idk, it just needs improvement.I liked the creative approach of making an open-world game set inside a big city, but what an absolutely boring and repetitive city this is. Everything looks the same and feels too pristine, in a doctor’s-office kind of way. Textures are repeated everywhere, and if you don’t have a Switch 2, good luck dealing with the pixelation in the ground tiles throughout the entire city.I liked the idea of jumping puzzles, but again, these felt rushed. Why couldn’t our character just be able to jump? I really don’t care for the lack of mobility in an “open-world” setting.Lastly, and what really drove me to write this review, is the stupidly horrible storytelling and writing. I get that this is intended for kids, but give them at least a little more credit. The premise starts off on an unrealistic note and just keeps spiraling downward. I will give the game credit for giving all the characters personality, but it ultimately went to waste. I was here for the Z-to-A grind, but somehow they made even that awkward after a certain point (I won’t spoil anything). Near the end of the game, I was just spamming the A button, trying to get these cringey scenes over with so I could be **** yeah, I wanted this to be a great game—I really did—but Game Freak and Nintendo dropped the ball MEGA hard on this one.
Worst pokemon ever. Would never buy anything from nintendo again after this one

I had some hype for this game because Arceus (which I played for at least 60 hours) is my current favorite Pokémon game,and looking at some trailers I thought that the battle system looked pretty fun but after I played I was so wrong.

First, I need to mention how the graphics look straight out of the PS3 era — like Sonic 2006 — and you can see that from the screenshots I’m showing you. And worst of all, the game doesn’t even run properly on the Switch 1. It barely holds 30 FPS. Arceus, even though it was ugly as hell, at least ran smoothly at 30 FPS. There are already tons of videos showing ugly PNG textures and how especially the environments in this game are terribly made.

Now, let’s talk about the main flaw — there’s absolutely no gameplay variety. What you do in the first hour of the tutorial is exactly what you’ll repeat for the entire game. In the tutorial, you basically learn that you’ll catch Pokémon, complete some side quests, and at night fight trainers in what they call the ‘Battle Zone.’ I can tolerate games with limited variety, but not when the little variety they have is this boring.

Why is it boring, you ask? The new battle system (which is no longer turn-based) just doesn’t work for Pokémon. In every fight, you’ll probably just spam your strongest move (using type weaknesses like always), and then use weaker ones only so you don’t ‘lose your turn’ while waiting for the cooldown. Eventually, you realize there’s no real strategy — you just button-mash your way to victory. 

See now how this system is and clair obscur 33 we can agree that the second choice is a better choice by far.

The capture is equal to arceus so i have any kind of bad things to say about that.

Besides that, there aren’t any real quality-of-life improvements. Every night, when the Battle Zone starts, you have to go through the same sequence — and you can’t skip it. Also, every time you heal your Pokémon at the Poké Center, you have to sit through the full animation. And don’t even get me started on how many black screens you’ll see during cutscenes. The game seriously needs to feel more fluid. I just want to battle trainers without constant black screens — am I asking too much? Or is this the bare minimum for a franchise as big as Pokémon?

On top of that, there’s no bicycle or skates to improve your mobility. The only thing you get is a double jump that **** so bad I don’t even have words for it. Honestly, they deserve an award for managing to make something this bad.

The last negative point I want to mention is how underused the moves are outside of battle. They show a few areas that you can only access with Rock Smash, but they never build on that idea. They could’ve used Surf, Waterfall, or Fly to make you curious about exploring the world — but there’s zero incentive to do so.

To be fair, there are some things worth praising. The music is good (even if it repeats the main theme too often), the character designs are nice and most of them are pretty cool, and the story is actually decent — especially when compared to Sword/Shield or Scarlet/Violet.
Well this not the best Pokemon ever made, but it has clearly lots of potential for fun, and is at least interesting, not gonna talk about the dlc tho....

This donuts mechanic is terrible,have time to do the quests is trash.
New megas is OK, nothing awesome.

Like come on. This is a lazy, unfinished, boring, repetitive game. I can't believe they can charge $70 for a game like this. The open world is really small and underwhelming. The buildings use 2d textures for the balcony and windows and you can't even go inside the buildings in the city! The new combat system wasn't really that great. Combined with the online it is just a mess. The strad is to run around in circles and steal kills. Everyone uses the same exact team when in the regular pokemon games teams are very diverse. The story wasn't that good either. Hell, there wasn't even a bad guy. And if you don't have Nintendo online you are going to have to pay $20 if you want the Kalos starter megas. Also they announced $30 DLC  for the $70 game. That's $120 if you wanna complete the dex. This is just pure greed! The side quests also **** booty. Barely any of them are unique at all! Side quests are supposed to be unique challenges that are fun to do. Instead they are are all either collecting an item or "find this pokemon" and they are insanely easy. The map only takes around 7 minutes to go from point A to point B. And the NPCs just stand there. Like it is a city, and they are just standing still like they are just copy and pasted models that were never coded to move. Even Scarlet and Violet had moving NPCs! The buildings are also just copy and paste with no variety at all! The starter choices also make no sense whatsoever. 2 Johto starters and Tepig? Like this is just odd and doesn't follow the rules that Legends Arceus set. Can you even call ZA a legends game at this point? With the city being so small, unfinished and boring to move across, the starter choice makes no sense, and Legends ZA isn't even that far away in the timeline. And they had a lot of potential to do a legends game for Kalos! It could have given us more lore about Zygarde, we could have seen what Kalos looked like back then, and we could have seen the war in action! That makes me feel beyond dissapointed and I am starting to lose hope in Pokemon all together with the recent games not getting any better. In my opinion, this game is definitly not worth getting.

I don't know how they moved so far forward with Legends Arceus and then so far back with this game. I had some hopes for this game but it just ****. Also, selling cut content 2 months after the game released is wild.

An indirect follow up to one of the weakest games in the series, this title had a lot of lost time (and story) to make up for. What was delivered was a shallow identity crisis for a series that’s been struggling. Thrown into This world with the illusion of choice, with no narrative weight or context your character is the “chosen one” destined to save the city for some nebulous issue that it refuses to tell you about until later in the game. The combat, while a fresh take, continues this illusion of choice and control- instead boiling down to the same formula the series has been trying to steer away from. Great character design is painfully juxtaposed against repetitive and mundane graphics that don’t lend themselves to the fantastical world the game is trying to sell you on. All the factors had me stopping the game four hours in to play a different jrpg that had better narrative weight and gameplay.

Pokémon Legends: Z.A delivers above-average gameplay for its genre. The combat system is dynamic and responsive, making battles fun and encouraging experimentation. Exploration is also enjoyable and successfully conveys the sense of freedom and novelty that the Legends series aims to provide. The soundtrack is another highlight, adding charm and atmosphere to the adventure in Lumiose City.Unfortunately, the game suffers heavily on a technical level. For a title developed for a new-generation console—especially considering the potential of the Nintendo Switch 2—the visuals are extremely disappointing. Buildings look flat and lack proper geometry, often resembling low-quality PNG images, which severely hurts immersion.Many assets, including Pokémon and environmental objects, feel graphically outdated, comparable to PlayStation 2 or early PlayStation 3-era games. This technical shortcoming overshadows what could have been a major step forward for the **** absence of voice acting is also a major drawback. Nintendo, Game Freak, and Creatures Inc. once again fail to meet modern industry standards by not offering voiced characters, at least in English and Japanese. Additionally, the lack of Brazilian Portuguese subtitles is frustrating, especially considering Brazil is one of Pokémon’s largest audiences.Overall, Pokémon Legends: Z.A has solid ideas, engaging gameplay, and a strong soundtrack, but is held back by outdated technical choices and questionable production decisions. It feels like a missed opportunity to fully realize both the game’s and the console’s potential.

Extremely medicore, I really thought they had kind of a comeback with Arceus and to a lesser extend with SV. The combat is kind of fun in a flashy and time saving kind of way, but even that barely works. The city setting could work if we had much better visual variety. I enjoy online combat, even if it's not even close to as engaging as the usual. If you don't think you gonna care for the combat though, don't bother.

I have never had the need to force myself to finish a Pokemon game. This however was the first time. The lack of exploration, meaningful side quests and even the lackluster main story like failed to capture my interest. The only thing worthwhile about this game is the new megas and some new favorite characters. That's about it though. Probably one of the worst Pokemon games in the franchise.
If i had to make a list of the worst pokemon games ever made, this would be the second one right after sword and shield base game

Loved it. It was my first experience with pokemon since pokemon red that I played on a nokia N95 phone via emulator. SinceI got this pokemon, I can't swap the game for another on my switch 2, really excited to finish the game and try the online gameplay.
I love the combat changes, but I felt claustrophobic being trapped in the city forever.

First things first: if you like the typical Pokémon formula, you’re probably going to like this game. New trainer in an unfamiliar place, gets a starter Pokémon, goes off to explore — for the longest time that was enough for me. I never really minded the formula because it worked.

But even though this game technically follows the same structure, I couldn’t shake the feeling that something was off while playing. For me, Pokémon has always been about discovering new creatures and exploring a new world. And to be fair, the new Megas are pretty good, and the new battle system is genuinely amazing.

The exploration, though, is where it loses me. Even though the game takes place within a single city, the areas don’t feel varied at all. Interacting with Pokémon doesn’t feel natural the way it did in Arceus or Scarlet/Violet. Movement mechanics like jumping and dodging feel clunky. There are tons of missions, but they come across more like repetitive chores — especially the main ones — and the story does a pretty poor job of tying everything together.

I’m not really the type to complain about Pokémon games. I know I’ll always end up buying them. But this time, I decided not to get the DLC because the positives were heavily overshadowed by the negatives. Honestly, for the first time ever, I didn’t buy a Pokémon game the moment it was released.

I’m not trying to convince anyone not to buy it — like I said at the start, if you enjoy the traditional Pokémon formula, you’ll probably enjoy this game. What I am saying is that instead of having the fandom constantly split over whether it’s good or bad, we should stop turning a blind eye to the elements that clearly hold it back from being a great game.
Forget whatever the internet is yelling about — if you truly enjoy the franchise, go play Pokémon Legends: ZA. It’s easily the best Pokémon game ever made.

The new non turn-based gameplay is pretty fun and smooth, and the story is great and the characters are really enjoyable and interesting (I really love the characters and plot). It also has a good mix of options with gameplay going between fighting trainers, catching Pokémon, and fighting rouge mega evolved Pokémon. It’s also really fun seeing all the different new mega evolved Pokémon. 

Also all the side quests are all super fun and really different! They have you doing all kinds of different things with different little plots and some of them end up giving you a pokemon that’ll have a story behind it now! I love it!

The whole game does take place in one city, but it never really bothered me because there’s so much to do, and plenty of different looking areas to explore, and it being in just one city allowed them to spend time refining everything else.

Also also the final fight is by far the best a Pokémon game has ever had.

My only real critique is that the main story progression takes a little too long, but also I had fun the whole time so eh lol.

Another missed opportunity, and we'll definitely never get a good Pokémon game again, judging by people's complacency. The only positive thing is the gameplay. Those who actually say it has a good story and good characters clearly haven't played another JRPG in their lives. The first 25 hours are just repeating the same thing, with a practically nonexistent story and characters who at least aren't complete idiots. What pisses me off is that in the last 5 hours they actually tell you some interesting things, the characters are important, and there are even some cool cutscenes. And I ask myself... WHY THE HELL COULDN'T THE REST OF THE GAME BE LIKE THE LAST 5 HOURS? I'm so fed up with Nintendo, The Pokémon Company, and Game Freak. Oh, and I'll skip over how awful the game is because everyone sees it, but the fact that it still doesn't have voice acting in 2025 is a **** disgrace.

The beginning of the game, at least to me, was very fun. I love the change in the battle system, and the characters are enjoyable. However, the mid portion, and especially the post game is very lack luster. I wish there was more in the base game without a DLC. Otherwise its fun.
Pokemon fans complain about every game, maybe skip a game or two. This game is Fire  and I can’t put it down.

Game is an unoptimized mess, even on Switch 2 which shows how little effort they put into optimization 
The gameplay is mediocre, the story is good
For $70 you’d expect more since it’s the same price of games like Silent Hill or Battlefield 6
But you’re not getting that kind of quality from this game

Pokémon Legends ZA is a really great game. The new battle mechanic feels fresh and adds so much excitement. Visually it is the best-looking Pokémon game so far, even if some buildings and windows could use more detail. The only thing is the story can feel a bit repetitive at times, but overall it is a fun, engaging experience and a strong step forward for the series.

This game is enjoyable, and hits some marks for me as a fan of the previous legends game. However this game misses too many marks for me to give it higher than a 6/10. I would suggest people still play this game, but maybe when they are taking a break from other more enjoyable titles.

The gameplay loop in Pokemon Legends: Z-A is quite enjoyable. There are definitely some moments throughout the game that could’ve had some fluff trimmed off to condense the experience, but overall I had a great time. However, it is hard to overlook the graphical design choices in this game. They aren’t bad and visuals aren’t everything, but the immersion is constantly broken by the silly decisions made by GameFreak and Nintendo. They didn’t fully push me away, but I could see it being a huge issue for others. The world itself also feels dramatically small compared to what I expected. Even before unlocking anything in the game beyond what was already there at the start, I felt like I explored everything the game offered.

While the combat is a good step up from the standard (very fast and quick) the poor visuals are unexcusable.

A game from the #1 franchise in profit should NOT look this bad.
All the textures of the building have no tesselation, no reflections of the sun etc.
Even certain areas, that are not part of the open city, look way better than all of the outside.

Do not support these garbage.

Very fun enjoyable game. Is it the best Pokemon game? No, but i feel it's overhated. Many people hate the buildings because they're flat, but if they were detailed, auto glide would be way too OP, if they made it detailed auto glide spamming could break the exploration of the game, and if they set barriers people would get mad for crashing into nothing. Gamefreak's Legends series is one of the most innovative things they've made, nothing feels old. Sure it's annoying that the game is only in Lumiose city, but you still get 20 wild zones and there is always people around making it feel alive and like a real city. I think the story is very well executed, of course there are some things i don't like with the game but most of it is minor things. The charecters are good too, i feel that Pokemon Legends ZA has been criticized by the people who always put pressure on game Studios to always meet up with expectations, this game is highly enjoyable. Loved it 9/10.

This is a fine example of why user review scores are reactionary and untrustworthy. Legends Z-A is a great Pokémon game, with fresh combat, fun exploration, and a charming story. It doesn't look great visually, but visuals aren't everything. Likely my favorite Pokémon release since ORAS.

Pokemon Legends ZA is a great demonstration of Game Freak's capability and decision making. Their choice to commit to their strengths paid dividends, even if the environment holds back a greater adventure. Its easy to appreciate a game where everything comes together, but recognizing the limits, and choosing to make the best experience possible within those constraints also matters.	'Gameplay first' is the Nintendo mantra, and remaining true to that philosophy has made Legends ZA a worthy follow up to the groundbreaking Legends Arceus. Though there is still work ahead to fully realize the potential of the franchise, Game Freak and Pokemon fans can be proud of this stride forward.

Pokemon Legends Z-A is a good game in my opinion. While it does have its short comings such as the flat buildings, it is overall enjoyable. The story, soundtrack, designs, and dialogue are all quite good, and the gameplay is fantastic.

It feel like it's able to show you what the anime has been doing for years as a playable game. It starts off slow in the early game, but becomes a lot quicker as the game progresses, leading to the game being very fun. It has room for improvements such as the lack of abilities, but it's still good, which speaks to the game's quality overall.

An unexpectedly good game. Despite what everyone seems to be saying about the graphics, they really don't impact on the experience much, at least for me. I'm no professional critic and I'm just basing this review on how fun the game was to play. The characters are SO well developed, the new megas are (mostly) cool and the gameplay arc is strangely fun and addictive, making you want to keep playing. The combat was an interesting new twist and definitely freshened up the usual formula. Even after finishing the main story I still wanted to keep playing, something that I don't always want to do after a Pokémon game. This game was a truly enjoyable experience and fully worth the money paid, at least on the switch 2. I continue to be surprised that people are willing to rate this game so lowly simply because the buildings are not high definition. This is hardly an important aspect of the game and I know it's really important to some people but don't let that put you off from a genuinely fun game.
